end_to_end_latency.description: Total Latency: time from when data is written to the source until it is written to the destination (GCP official definition)

stream_processing_latency.description: Stream Latency: time from when Datastream reads the event until it writes to the destination (GCP official definition)

system_latency.description: System Latency: time from when Datastream reads the event until it writes to the destination — similar to stream latency (GCP official definition)

freshness.description: Freshness: difference between when data was committed to source and when Datastream reads it. 0 if caught up. Formula: Total Latency = Freshness + System Latency (GCP official definition)
freshness.note: Almost entirely 0 — stream is keeping up with source

latency_breakdown.note: Total Latency = Freshness + System Latency (per GCP docs). 99.4% of total latency is Freshness (read lag from Oracle LogMiner archived redo logs), only 0.6% is Datastream processing.
key_factors_affecting_read_lag: Oracle redo log archival frequency (primary factor — aim for 10-20 min switches)
LogMiner is single-threaded (consider Binary log reader if available)
Redo log file size (smaller = more frequent switches = lower latency)
Oracle source health (CPU, SGA/PGA sizing, disk I/O)
Oracle 19c deprecated CONTINUOUS_MINE — archived log mode only
latency_breakdown.gcp_expected_latency: Up to 30 min end-to-end (per GCP docs). Observed avg of 66 min suggests redo log switch frequency can be optimized.
